activities_projects.title: Mapping the Reflex Arc
activities_projects.objective: Understand the pathway of a nerve impulse during a reflex action
steps: Identify a stimulus (e.g., touching a hot object).
Draw the sensory neuron carrying the signal to the spinal cord.
Draw the relay neuron within the spinal cord.
Draw the motor neuron carrying the signal to the effector muscle.
Label the 'Reflex Arc' and explain why the brain is informed later.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Visualizes the structural components of a reflex arc and explains the speed of involuntary responses.
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct labeling of receptor, sensory neuron, spinal cord, motor neuron, and effector (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Effect of Auxin on Plant Growth
activities_projects.objective: Demonstrate how unequal distribution of hormones causes bending in plants
steps: Place both plants in a box with light coming from only one side.
Cover the tip of Plant A with a small cap of aluminum foil.
Leave the tip of Plant B uncovered.
Observe the growth after 48 hours.
Discuss why Plant B bent toward the light while Plant A grew straight.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that auxins synthesized at the shoot tip are sensitive to light and migrate to the shady side.
activities_projects.evaluation: Explanation of the role of auxins in cell elongation on the shaded side of the stem (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Chemical Coordination: The Sugar Balance
activities_projects.objective: Understand the feedback mechanism of Insulin secretion
steps: Research the role of the Pancreas in detecting blood sugar levels.
Create a flowchart showing what happens when blood sugar rises after a meal.
Show the 'switch-off' mechanism once sugar levels return to normal.
Identify the hormone (Glucagon) that acts when sugar levels are too low.
Define 'Feedback Mechanism' in the context of the endocrine system.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Explains how the body maintains homeostasis through self-regulating hormonal signals.
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of the feedback loop diagram for insulin secretion (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Response of Tendrils to Touch
materials: Pea or Money plant with tendrils
steps: Place a support stick near a young, growing tendril.
Ensure the tendril makes contact with the stick.
Observe the growth pattern of the tendril over the next 2-3 days.
Notice how the part of the tendril in contact with the stick grows slower than the outer part.
Record the direction of the coil.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes thigmotropism as a growth-dependent movement in response to touch.
activities_projects.evaluation: Explanation of how differential growth rates cause the tendril to circle the support (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Chemotropism Investigation
activities_projects.objective: Understand how chemicals guide plant reproduction
steps: Place a few drops of sugar solution on a slide.
Dust some pollen grains into the solution.
Wait for 15-30 minutes and observe under the microscope.
Look for the growth of pollen tubes.
Relate this to how the ovary's chemicals guide the pollen tube in a flower.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Defines chemotropism and its significance in the fertilization process of flowering plants.
activities_projects.evaluation: Sketch of the germinating pollen grain with a visible pollen tube (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Comparing Voluntary and Involuntary Actions
activities_projects.objective: Distinguish between actions controlled by the Cerebrum and the Medulla/Spinal Cord
steps: List 10 daily activities (e.g., walking, heartbeat, breathing, writing, sneezing).
Classify them into 'Voluntary', 'Involuntary', and 'Reflex Actions'.
Identify which part of the brain or nervous system controls each category.
Explain why we cannot consciously control the peristaltic movement of the gut.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Differentiates the roles of the forebrain (conscious thought) and the hindbrain/spinal cord (survival functions).
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct classification of at least 8 out of 10 activities (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Master Gland and Growth
activities_projects.objective: Research the impact of Pituitary hormones on development
materials: Case studies (images of Gigantism or Dwarfism from textbook/internet)
steps: Identify the Pituitary gland's location in the brain.
Research 'Growth Hormone' and its functions.
Analyze what happens when there is an oversecretion (Gigantism) during childhood.
Analyze what happens during undersecretion (Dwarfism).
Discuss why it is called the 'Master Gland'.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the profound effect of the endocrine system on physical structure and development.
activities_projects.evaluation: Summary report on the correlation between hormone levels and growth disorders (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Iodine and the Thyroid
materials: Packets of common salt (Iodized vs. Non-iodized labels)
steps: Examine the label of a salt packet for the word 'Iodized'.
Research why Iodine is necessary for the Thyroid gland to produce Thyroxin.
Explain the role of Thyroxin in metabolism (carbohydrates, proteins, fats).
Describe the symptoms of Goitre and why it is prevalent in hilly areas.
Write a short awareness slogan for using iodized salt.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Connects nutritional intake to the proper functioning of the endocrine system.
activities_projects.evaluation: Explanation of the link between Iodine, Thyroxin, and Goitre (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Adrenaline: The Emergency System
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the systemic changes during stress
materials: Research data on heart rate and blood flow during stress
steps: List the target organs of Adrenaline (Heart, Lungs, Liver, Digestive system).
Explain why blood is diverted from the skin and digestive system to the skeletal muscles.
Describe how the increase in breathing rate and heart rate benefits the body in a 'fight' situation.
Draw a diagram showing the 'Adrenaline Rush' pathway.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Describes the integrated response of the body to chemical signals from the adrenal glands.
activities_projects.evaluation: List of at least four physiological changes caused by adrenaline and their purposes (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Phytohormones Comparison
activities_projects.objective: Differentiate between growth-promoting and growth-inhibiting hormones
steps: Make four cards for: Auxin, Gibberellin, Cytokinin, and Abscisic Acid.
List one major function for each (e.g., Cytokinin for cell division, Abscisic acid for wilting leaves).
Group them into 'Promoters' and 'Inhibitors'.
Identify which hormone is responsible for the ripening of fruits.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Categorizes plant hormones based on their regulatory effects on the plant life cycle.
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct matching of all four hormones to their primary functions (10 marks)
